title: Complex Data Structures

Complex Data Structures

Complex Data Structures

Complex data usually features one or more levels of nested data and is often represented as a tree-like structure. Uniface component structures, XML data, and JSON strings are typical examples of complex data structures.

Uniface Component Structures

The Structure pane of the Uniface Component Editor shows the component structure as a tree.

Note: The actual runtime component structure is more complex, because the Structure pane does not contain the real data or represent entity occurrences.

XML and XHTML

XML and XHTML data is also complex, and can include constructs for which Uniface has no equivalents. Consider the following XML code:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>  [1]
<book>  [2]
 <title>Installation Guide</title>
 [3]
 <author>Rocket Software B.V.</author>
 <frontmatter>
    <toc/> [4]     <!– Generated Table of Contents →  [5]
    <preface>This book describes how to install the product.</preface>
 </frontmatter>
 <chapter> [6]
    <title>Requirements</title>
    <p>Before installing the components, make a plan for what you need 
       to accomplish and the components that will support these goals.</p>
    <p>After installing the components, you will probably want to perform
       some preliminary configuration. </p>
	</chapter>
 <chapter> [6]
    <title>Installation</title>
    <section>
      <title>Attach the drive to the system</title>
      <note type="caution"> [7]  You must be grounded before doing this.</note>
      <ol>
        <li>Find an empty bay for your drive.</li>
        <li>Remove the drive from its electrostatic bag.</li>
        <li>Screw the drive in to the frame.</li>
        <li>Attach the multi-colored power cord to the drive.</li>
        <li>Attach the cable that connects your hard drive to the motherboard.
            <p>See the Figure 5.</p></li> </ol> [8]
    </section>
 </chapter>
…
</book>
  1. XML declaration.
  2. XML element containing other elements. (book contains title, author, frontmatter, and multiple chapters).
  3. XML element contains text data.
  4. XML element defined, but contains no data.
  5. XML comment; its content is related to its position in the document.
  6. Multiple elements with same name.
  7. XML element has attribute that contains text data. (type = "caution")
  8. XML element contains both text data and other elements.

Transformations

Uniface, XML, and JSON use constructs that cannot be directly interpreted by the other format. They need to be transformed, and the way in which they are transformed can vary a great deal. As a developer, you therefore need complete control over how to manipulate and convert the data.

To transform complex data from one format to another, or to extract specific data from it, you need to assign the data to a variable or parameter of type struct, and manipulate it using ProcScript operators and functions.

Related Links
  • Structs

    A Struct is a tree-like data structure in memory that is used to dynamically manipulate complex data and transform it from or to XML, JSON, or Uniface component data. A Struct can be addressed by assigning it to a variable, parameter, or non-database field of type struct, and manipulated using ProcScript commands, access operators, and information functions. For information on using Structs, see Transforming Complex Data Using StructsYou can dynamically transform complex data structures, such as XML, JSON, or Uniface component structures, into another format using in-memory data structures called Structs. .

  • Assigning Values to Structs

    Structs are assigned to Structs either by reference or by value.

  • ProcScript for Manipulating Structs

    The ProcScript language provides a struct data type, ProcScript statements and functions, as well as access operators and Struct functions that enable you to manipulate data in a Struct and transform it into other data formats.

After installing the components, you will probably want to perform some preliminary configuration.

[ 6 ] Installation
Attach the drive to the system [ 7 ] You must be grounded before doing this.
  1. Find an empty bay for your drive.
  2. Remove the drive from its electrostatic bag.
  3. Screw the drive in to the frame.
  4. Attach the multi-colored power cord to the drive.
  5. Attach the cable that connects your hard drive to the motherboard.

    See the Figure 5.

[ 8 ]
